---
title: "1.12 Objects: Instances of Classes"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Classes and Objects

### Blueprint and instance

![Blueprint and cookie-cutter model](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/blueprint.png){width="36%"}
![Cookie-cutter model](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/cookieCutterLabelled.png){width="52%"}

A <span class="term">class</span> is like a blueprint or cookie cutter.  
An <span class="term">object</span> is a specific thing created from that class.

Objects are <span class="term">instances</span> of the class.

## Attributes and Behaviors

### What an object knows and what it can do

![Cats as objects with shared structure](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/catsLabelled.png){fig-align="center" width="68%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">attributes</span> are object data or properties
- <span class="term">behaviors</span> are actions, implemented by methods
:::

Different cat objects have different values, but the same broad structure.

## Turtle as an Object Model

### Concrete object-oriented vocabulary

![Turtle attributes and methods](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/turtleOOD.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"}

The `Turtle` class is useful because students can see object state and behavior visually:

::: {.tight-list}
- color, size, and position are attributes
- `forward()`, `turnLeft()`, `turnRight()` are behaviors
:::

## Reference Variables

### A turtle variable refers to an object

```java
Turtle yertle;
Turtle myrtle;
```

Variables of class types store <span class="term">references</span>, not primitive values.  
That is why objects can carry many attributes together as one unit.

## Creating Multiple Objects

### One class, many instances

```java
World habitat = new World(300, 300);
Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);
Turtle myrtle = new Turtle(habitat);
```

One class can create <span class="mark">as many objects as needed</span>.  
Each object has its own state and can behave independently.

## Inheritance

### A class hierarchy can share structure

![Inheritance hierarchy](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/inheritance-hierarchy-pet.png){fig-align="center" width="62%"}

The textbook keeps inheritance here as vocabulary context:

::: {.tight-list}
- superclass
- subclass
- inherited attributes and behaviors
:::

It is not the main AP focus anymore, but it helps explain class relationships.

## Turtle Methods and Coordinates

### Visual reasoning matters

::: {.two-col}
![Turtle UML class diagram](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/turtleUMLClassDiagram.png){width="100%"}
![Turtle coordinate system](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/coords.png){width="88%"}
:::

The turtle world uses screen coordinates:

::: {.tight-list}
- `(0,0)` is at the top-left
- `x` increases to the right
- `y` increases downward
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- class/object/attribute/behavior vocabulary checks
- turtle movement edits and extra-object creation
- coordinate-aware drawing tasks
- <span class="term">1.12.7 Coding Challenge: Draw Letters</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- define class, object, and instance correctly
- distinguish attributes from behaviors
- explain that reference variables point to objects
- recognize that one class can create many objects
- read the turtle coordinate system correctly
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
